text.title: RAG Corpus Discovery Interview — Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every application of `rag-corpus-discovery-interview` must enforce. The rules pin the discipline that turns a interview from folklore into a reviewable, owned, version-controlled operating tool.

rule.statement: The interview is run from a written guide (≤2 pages) with 5–9 prompts. Guide is reviewed against the goal before the call.
rule.rationale: Unguided interviews drift into chat and miss the goal. A written guide is the cheap discipline that makes one interview comparable to the next.

rule.statement: Questions anchor on recent specific events ('last time you tried…') not opinions ('would you use…').

rationale.source: Mom Test; Christensen JTBD interview practice

rule.rationale: Opinion answers predict nothing. Past-behaviour answers predict future behaviour. This rule is the single biggest interview-quality lever.

rule.statement: Interviews are recorded (with consent) and transcribed within 48 hours. Notes alone are not sufficient evidence.
rule.rationale: Memory of an interview decays sharply; the transcript is the durable artefact. Tagging requires verbatim text.

rule.statement: No synthesis claims are made on < 5 interviews. Single-interview insights are tagged 'hypothesis', not 'finding'.

rationale.source: User-research saturation studies; Christensen JTBD discipline

rule.rationale: n=1 quotes turn into product roadmaps and burn quarters. Five is the cheapest threshold where patterns start to appear.

rule.statement: Prompts are reviewed for leading phrasing before the call. 'Would you pay $X for…' is rejected; 'walk me through what you currently pay for…' is accepted.
rule.rationale: Leading prompts contaminate the data and the founder believes their own hypothesis. Non-leading prompts surface what the respondent actually does.
